questions.question: Why do file sync services split files into fixed-size blocks instead of uploading files whole?
options: So that only the changed blocks need to be transferred on subsequent updates, saving bandwidth.
So that the metadata service can skip checksumming entirely.
Because object stores cannot accept files larger than 4 MB.
Because splitting enables cross-device locking on individual blocks.
questions.explanation: Block-level delta sync is the core bandwidth optimization: only changed blocks are uploaded, not the full file.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of content-addressing (hashing blocks by content) in a file sync system?
options: It enables deduplication: identical blocks stored by any user map to the same object, stored once.
It allows the client to skip authentication for block uploads.
It ensures block ordering is preserved during network reordering.
It allows the metadata service to avoid storing block lists.
questions.explanation: Content-addressing means the hash is both the key and the identity proof, enabling cross-user deduplication without extra bookkeeping.

questions.question: Two devices edit the same file concurrently while one is offline. When the offline device reconnects and commits, what should the metadata service do?
options: Silently overwrite the server version with the offline device's version.
Reject the offline device's commit and return an error with no conflict copy.
Detect the parent version mismatch and create a conflict copy, giving both devices both versions.
Merge the two file versions byte-by-byte automatically.
questions.explanation: Creating a conflict copy is the safe default for file sync: no data is lost, and the user can resolve the conflict manually.

questions.question: Which design decision makes cross-user block deduplication impossible?
options: Storing blocks in a separate object store from metadata.
End-to-end encryption where only the client holds the encryption key.
Using SHA-256 hashes as block identifiers.
Limiting file size to 5 GB.
questions.explanation: With client-side encryption, two users who have the same file produce different ciphertexts with different hashes, eliminating cross-user dedup.

questions.question: A block garbage collector deletes blocks that have zero references. What race condition must you prevent?
options: A new file version commits a block reference while GC is simultaneously deleting that block.
Two clients uploading the same block at the same time.
The CDN serving a stale block hash to the client.
The notification service delivering sync events out of order.
questions.explanation: The GC must use a tombstone or two-phase delete window to avoid deleting a block that a concurrent version commit is about to reference.

questions.question: Which metric is the best early warning that sync is falling behind for connected devices?
options: The delta sync latency p95 from file save on one device to availability on another.
The total number of blocks stored in object storage.
The number of API server instances in the fleet.
The average file size across all users.
questions.explanation: End-to-end delta sync latency directly measures the user-visible outcome and catches problems anywhere in the pipeline.

questions.question: What trade-off does a sync-cursor-based delta API offer compared to polling all file metadata on each sync?
options: Cursors allow devices to fetch only events they have not yet seen, reducing server load and bandwidth per sync cycle.
Cursors guarantee exactly-once delivery without any client-side deduplication.
Cursors remove the need for block-level checksums.
Cursors allow the server to push blocks to devices without a pull request.
questions.explanation: Incremental cursor-based sync amortizes the cost of listing all changes; only the delta since the last known position is returned.

questions.question: A user reconnects after two weeks offline. Their device has thousands of locally changed files. What server-side risk does this create and how do you mitigate it?
options: The device sends a block-check batch for thousands of files simultaneously, potentially overloading the metadata service — mitigate with per-user rate limiting and staggered reconnect backoff.
The block storage runs out of space — mitigate by pre-allocating 1 TB per device.
The notification service cannot identify the device — mitigate by rotating device tokens weekly.
The CDN serves stale blocks — mitigate by purging the entire CDN on each reconnect.
questions.explanation: Mass reconnect creates a thundering-herd on metadata and block-check APIs; rate limiting and backoff protect the service.
